title: Vegetarian mulligatawny
subtitle: Anglo-Indian lentil and vegetable pepper-water soup
excerpt: A thick, warming soup of red lentils, carrot, and apple in a spiced onion base, finished with coconut milk and a squeeze of lemon. The Anglo-Indian adaptation of the Tamil milagu tannir (pepper water), transformed by colonial kitchens into a hearty meal starter.
sourceNotes: Based on Anglo-Indian mulligatawny as documented in Lizzie Collingham, 'Curry: A Tale of Cooks and Conquerors' (2005) and Madhur Jaffrey, 'A Taste of India' (1985). The vegetarian version follows the soup's older form more closely than the later meat-enriched variants, using lentils as the primary protein and retaining the sourness that characterises the Tamil original.

content.text: Mulligatawny began as milagu tannir (pepper water), a thin, sour, spiced broth from Tamil Nadu. It was used as a digestive and a condiment. British colonial officers picked it up. They thickened it with lentils and meat, sweetened it with apple and onion, and sent it home to England as the main soup of the Anglo-Indian table. By the nineteenth century it was in every British cookery book and club menu, often a long way from the first dish.

This version is vegetarian and leans closer to the soup's southern Indian roots. Red lentils give it body. A spiced base of onion, carrot, and apple builds the flavour. Coconut milk adds richness. Lemon sharpens the finish. It is faster and lighter than the chicken or lamb kind, and works as a starter or as a bowl with bread.

items.name: Cooking apple
items.prepNote: peeled and diced; Bramley or any tart apple

content.text: Heat {{sunflower-oil}} of oil in a large saucepan over a medium heat. Add {{onion}} diced onion and cook for 8 minutes until softened and lightly golden. Add {{garlic}} crushed garlic, {{ginger-fresh}} grated ginger, {{curry-powder}} of curry powder, and {{turmeric-ground}} of turmeric. Stir for 2 minutes until fragrant.

Add {{carrot}} diced carrot, {{cooking-apple}} diced apple, and {{red-lentils}} of rinsed red lentils. Pour in {{vegetable-stock}} of vegetable stock and season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to the boil, reduce the heat, and simmer for 20 to 25 minutes until the lentils and vegetables are completely soft. Stir in {{coconut-milk}} of coconut milk and {{lemon}} of lemon juice. Taste and adjust the seasoning. Serve hot, the soup should be thick but pourable; add a little more stock if needed.

items.fix: Add more stock or boiling water and stir to combine. Mulligatawny should be thick but flowing, not a porridge.
items.cause: Lentils have absorbed all the stock

items.fix: Add more lemon juice, the soup needs real sharpness to read correctly. Taste after adding more salt as well, as both work together.
The coconut milk softens heat. Start with 1 tablespoon of curry powder if using a hot blend, and add the second tablespoon only after tasting.
items.cause: Curry powder too hot for the amount used

content.text: Mulligatawny was the great Anglo-Indian import to the Victorian table. It was the first curry many British people ever tasted, served in club dining rooms and middle-class homes through the nineteenth century. It appears in every major Victorian cookery manual, from Mrs Beeton onwards, usually with meat added. The vegetarian version goes back closer to the Tamil pepper-water root, which was meatless and meant as a digestive, before it was enriched and turned into the thick British soup of today.
